programs.name: North Dakota Aging in Community (AIC) Project
programs.tagline: Community-based support to help your parent age in place in select rural North Dakota communities.

localEntities.name: NDSU Extension leading, with community stakeholders in two rural communities

programs.intro:
If your parent lives in one of two rural North Dakota communities and wants to stay in their home as they age, the Aging in Community (AIC) Project may provide tailored local solutions. This project focuses on addressing specific challenges rural older adults face — like limited transportation, social isolation, housing concerns, and access to health services.

Unlike traditional benefit programs with fixed payments, AIC develops community-specific interventions based on local needs. Because it's run through NDSU Extension with community partners, solutions are designed around what each rural community actually needs rather than one-size-fits-all services.

This is currently available in only two rural North Dakota communities (specific locations not publicly specified). The project emphasizes community engagement over individual applications.

summary: Lives in one of two targeted rural communities
otherRequirements: Must reside in one of the two targeted rural communities
Commitment to community-focused solutions and engagement

applicationGuide.summary: Contact NDSU Extension or engage through local community stakeholders — no standard application process.

steps.title: Check community availability
steps.description: Confirm your parent lives in one of the two rural communities currently participating in the AIC project.

steps.title: Contact NDSU Extension
steps.description: Visit the NDSU Extension AIC program page or contact them directly to learn about participation in your community.

steps.title: Engage with local stakeholders
steps.description: Participate in community meetings or assessments where local aging-in-place solutions are being developed.

applicationGuide.tip: This isn't a traditional benefit program with individual applications — it's about community-wide solutions, so engagement through local meetings may be more effective than trying to apply individually.

contentSections.text: This is a community development project, not a direct service program. If your parent needs immediate financial assistance or personal care services, also explore North Dakota SPED (Senior Programs and Enhanced Delivery) or Medicaid Home and Community-Based Services.

faqs.question: How do I know if my parent's community is one of the two participating?
faqs.answer: The specific community names aren't publicly listed. Contact NDSU Extension at the program website to confirm if your parent's rural community is participating in the AIC project.

faqs.question: What kind of help can my parent actually get through this program?
faqs.answer: Services vary by community needs but may include transportation solutions, social connection programs, housing support, health and wellness activities, and personal finance assistance. Each community develops different interventions based on local assessments.

faqs.question: Is this the same as other North Dakota aging programs like SPED?
faqs.answer: No. SPED provides direct payments and respite care to individuals who qualify. AIC is a community development project that creates local solutions but doesn't provide individual cash benefits or personal care services.

faqs.question: Does my parent need to meet income or asset limits?
faqs.answer: No specific income or asset limits are defined for the AIC project since it focuses on community-wide solutions rather than individual financial assistance.

faqs.question: What if my parent's rural community isn't participating?
faqs.answer: Currently only two communities participate. You can contact NDSU Extension to express interest, but also explore other North Dakota aging programs like SPED, Medicaid HCBS, or local Area Agencies on Aging services.

faqs.question: How long will this program be available?
faqs.answer: As a project-based initiative, duration isn't specified. Contact NDSU Extension for current status and whether the program may expand to additional communities.

programs.name: Senior Safety Program (ND Assistive)
programs.tagline: Free safety devices and professional setup to help your parent stay safely at home in North Dakota.
programs.intro:
If your parent is 60+ and living at home in North Dakota, they can get free safety devices through the Senior Safety Program. This includes alerting devices for hearing loss, anti-elopement devices (to prevent wandering), and other home safety equipment — all with professional setup assistance.

Because the program provides both the devices and installation help, your parent won't struggle with complicated setup or wonder if the equipment is working properly. The program is designed specifically for seniors who want to remain safely in their own homes rather than move to assisted living.

summary: Living at home (not in nursing facility)
otherRequirements: Cannot be living in a basic or skilled nursing facility

applicationGuide.summary: Apply online or email the completed form — no processing time specified.

steps.title: Complete the application
steps.description: Fill out the Request For North Dakota Assistive Safety Devices form online or download the PDF version.

steps.title: Submit your request
steps.description: Submit online through the ND Assistive website or email the completed form to seniorsafety@ndassistive.org.

steps.title: Program review
steps.description: ND Assistive will review your parent's specific safety needs and determine which devices are appropriate.

steps.title: Device delivery and setup
steps.description: Approved safety devices will be delivered with professional installation assistance.

applicationGuide.tip: The program offers both standard and large-print application forms if your parent has vision difficulties.

contentSections.text: This program is only for seniors living at home — your parent cannot be residing in a basic care or skilled nursing facility to qualify.

faqs.question: What types of safety devices does the program provide?
faqs.answer: The program provides alerting devices for hearing loss, anti-elopement devices (to prevent wandering for those with dementia), and other home safety devices. Contact the program directly to discuss your parent's specific safety needs.

faqs.question: Does my parent's income affect their eligibility?
faqs.answer: No. There are no income or asset limits for this program. Any North Dakota resident age 60+ living at home can qualify.

faqs.question: Will someone help install the safety equipment?
faqs.answer: Yes. The program includes professional setup assistance, so your parent won't have to figure out complicated installation on their own.

faqs.question: Can my parent apply if they live in assisted living?
faqs.answer: It depends on the level of care. They cannot be living in a basic care or skilled nursing facility, but the program may work with other living situations. Contact ND Assistive to clarify your parent's specific housing arrangement.

faqs.question: How do I know which safety devices my parent needs?
faqs.answer: After you submit the application, ND Assistive will review your parent's specific safety needs and recommend appropriate devices. You don't need to choose the equipment yourself.

faqs.question: Is there a limit to how many devices my parent can receive?
faqs.answer: The program materials don't specify limits on the number of devices. Contact seniorsafety@ndassistive.org to discuss your parent's particular safety needs and what the program can provide.

programs.name: Basic Care Assistance Program (BCAP)
programs.tagline: Helps pay room and board costs if your parent lives in a basic care facility and doesn't qualify for full Medicaid.

localEntities.name: Local Human Service Zone offices, county social workers

programs.intro:
If your parent lives in a licensed basic care facility in North Dakota and has limited income, BCAP may cover their room and board costs. This program fills a gap for seniors who need assisted living but don't qualify for full Medicaid benefits.

BCAP only works if your parent already lives in (or is moving to) a licensed basic care facility — it won't pay for home care or independent living. Your parent must be 65+ or disabled, be a North Dakota resident, and meet both financial and functional assessment requirements. Because BCAP ties into Medicaid eligibility rules, the application process involves extensive documentation and a personal care assessment.
programs.savingsSource: Room and board payment amount varies by facility and individual assessment

summary: Lives in licensed basic care facility
structuredEligibility.functionalRequirement: Must complete a Personal Care Services Assessment showing need for assistance with daily activities. Higher care levels (B/C) require Nursing Facility Level of Care assessment.
otherRequirements: Must live in a licensed Basic Care Facility in North Dakota
Must apply for Medicare if eligible
Must provide Social Security number and extensive documentation

applicationGuide.summary: Apply through your local Human Service Zone office using form SFN 405, with processing time not specified.

steps.title: Contact your local Human Service Zone office
steps.description: Find your county's office to start the application process and get the required forms.

steps.title: Complete Application for Assistance SFN 405
steps.description: Fill out sections 1, 3, 4, 6, and 8 specifically for BCAP. This covers your parent's basic information, residence, and financial details.

steps.title: Gather required documentation
steps.description: Collect proof of age, identity, residence, financial information, and disability status if applicable.

steps.title: Complete Personal Care Services Assessment
steps.description: A caseworker will assess your parent's functional needs to determine their care level (A, B, or C).

steps.title: Submit application and documents
steps.description: Turn in completed forms and documentation to your county social worker or Human Service Zone office.

applicationGuide.tip: If your parent already receives Medicaid, their financial eligibility will be determined through their Medicaid redetermination process.

urls.label: North Dakota Department of Human Services

contentSections.text: BCAP is specifically for basic care facility residents only. If your parent lives independently or in their own home, this program won't help — look into other North Dakota Medicaid programs instead.

faqs.question: What's the difference between BCAP and regular Medicaid?
faqs.answer: BCAP is for people who need some assistance but don't qualify for full Medicaid benefits. It only covers room and board costs in basic care facilities, while Medicaid covers comprehensive medical care and services.

faqs.question: Can Mom apply for BCAP if she's already in a basic care facility?
faqs.answer: Yes, she must already be living in a licensed basic care facility to qualify. BCAP won't help her move into a facility — she needs to be a resident first.

faqs.question: What if Mom's income is too high for BCAP but she can't afford the facility costs?
faqs.answer: Contact your local Human Service Zone office to explore other options. There may be different Medicaid programs or payment plans available depending on her specific situation.

faqs.question: How do they determine what care level Mom needs?
faqs.answer: A caseworker will complete a Personal Care Services Assessment looking at her ability to handle daily activities like bathing, dressing, and medication management. Higher care levels (B and C) require meeting Nursing Facility Level of Care criteria.

faqs.question: Can Mom keep her house if she qualifies for BCAP?
faqs.answer: The research doesn't specify BCAP's asset rules, but it follows Medicaid-style eligibility. Contact your local Human Service Zone office for details about how home ownership affects eligibility.

faqs.question: What happens if Mom moves to a different basic care facility?
faqs.answer: She'll need to notify her caseworker about the move. BCAP should continue as long as she moves to another licensed basic care facility in North Dakota and still meets all other requirements.
